american-airlines-starlink-deal.headline: American Airlines confirms Starlink deal, raising the stakes for rivals on legacy systems
american-airlines-starlink-deal.body_md:
American Airlines announced on Tuesday that it will install SpaceX's Starlink satellite internet across more than 500 of its narrowbody Airbus aircraft, beginning in the first quarter of 2027. The market took notice: AAL shares jumped nearly 7% on the day, putting the stock on track for a four-month high.

The deal covers American's Airbus fleet — A321neo and A321XLR variants — but conspicuously leaves its Boeing aircraft untouched for now. Those planes will continue running a patchwork of Viasat and Panasonic hardware. Starlink's Aero Terminal is rated at up to 1 gigabit per second per antenna, a meaningful step up from current satellite systems that typically deliver a fraction of that speed at significantly higher latency.

"The addition of Starlink solidifies American as a leading airline in keeping passengers connected in flight," said Heather Garboden, the carrier's chief customer officer. SpaceX's Jason Fritch, vice president of Starlink enterprise sales, called the partnership a path to "a fully connected experience gate to gate."

## The legacy gap

The competitive picture around American's announcement is arguably more significant than the deal itself. United Airlines has been rolling out Starlink since 2024 and expects to have 800 aircraft equipped by the end of 2026 — a lead American will spend years closing. Southwest and Alaska Airlines are also in the Starlink camp.

Delta Air Lines is the conspicuous holdout. After talks with SpaceX reportedly broke down over a branding dispute — Delta wanted to maintain its proprietary Delta Sync portal rather than surface the Starlink name — the carrier signed with Amazon's Project Kuiper instead. That bet carries a material timing problem: Amazon's Kuiper constellation has fewer than 250 satellites in orbit against the roughly 10,000 in Starlink's network, and Delta's installations are not expected to begin until 2028. Until then, Delta passengers fly on Viasat hardware rated at 12 to 150 megabits per second and latency between 500 and 700 milliseconds — a different product class than what United and, eventually, American will offer.

For investors, that gap translates into a valuation narrative. Airlines capable of marketing a premium, reliable connectivity product have an asset that is difficult to replicate quickly. Delta's two-year window of competitive disadvantage is a known risk, and the AA announcement extends the timeline over which that risk compounds.

The next data point to watch is American's Boeing fleet. The carrier has not indicated when — or whether — it intends to extend Starlink to those planes, leaving the rollout picture incomplete. With installations not starting until early 2027, the first real-world passenger experience numbers are still roughly a year away.

sls-leukemia-gps-nccn-pathway.headline: SLS chief points to leukaemia trial data as path to NCCN guideline recognition
sls-leukemia-gps-nccn-pathway.body_md:
Shares of SELLAS Life Sciences (Nasdaq: SLS) extended an overnight gain on Wednesday after CEO Angelos Stergiou told investors he was "very bullish" about forthcoming Phase 3 trial data for the company's lead cancer immunotherapy, and said strong results could position the drug for inclusion in National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) treatment guidelines — a milestone with direct implications for how widely oncologists can prescribe and insurers will pay for it.

The stock rose roughly 6% on the day, part of a year-long run that has now carried SLS up more than 345%. No trial results have been publicly disclosed. The REGAL Phase 3 study, which tests Galinpepimut-S (GPS) as maintenance therapy for acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) patients in second complete remission, remains blinded. What has drawn investors is the pace at which the trial is accumulating the clinical events required to trigger its final analysis.

## Seventy-eight of eighty

REGAL is event-driven: final analysis begins once 80 patient deaths have been recorded across the two trial arms. As of 11 May, 78 events had been logged. Stergiou's argument — and it is an inferential one — is that longer-than-expected survival in the GPS arm is slowing event accrual. "80 events have not occurred yet," he told listeners, "which obviously indicates longer survival" than the trial's original design assumed. The company had projected a median overall survival of 12.6 months for GPS-treated patients against 8 months under best available therapy.

GPS targets the Wilms Tumor-1 (WT1) antigen, activating T cells to attack leukaemic stem cells. The appeal of the mechanism is that it is mutation-agnostic — relevant in AML, where targeted therapies face the constant threat of resistance through tumour evolution. Phase 2 data in first-remission patients showed a median overall survival of approximately 68 months, a figure Stergiou has described as "quite unprecedented" for the indication. Elderly patients — median age 74 — showed 21 months in earlier studies, against 5.4 months without GPS.

## Commentary as catalyst

What Stergiou specifically flagged for investors was the possibility that strong REGAL results could win GPS guideline recognition not only in the AML second-remission setting the Phase 3 trial directly tests, but in the adjacent first-remission (CR1) context as well. NCCN inclusion in that broader population would substantially widen the commercial case for a drug currently on the cusp of its first regulatory test.

The cautionary note writes itself. Wednesday's move was driven entirely by forward-looking executive commentary, with no trial data, no peer-reviewed manuscript, and no regulatory filing in view. Studies of NCCN panel behaviour suggest a median of roughly 15 months between a regimen's initial data presentation and guideline inclusion — and that clock does not start until topline data are actually in hand. Biotech investors know this pattern well: a CEO at a conference or investor forum provides just enough specificity to move a stock without furnishing the evidence that would allow independent scrutiny.

The next verifiable catalyst is the 80th event itself, which Stergiou has described as imminent, followed by topline data release. That readout will either validate a year's worth of pre-data optimism or compress those gains sharply. No formal timeline has been committed to by the company.
sls-leukemia-gps-nccn-pathway.in_one_sentence: SELLAS Life Sciences CEO says imminent REGAL trial readout could put GPS immunotherapy on a path to NCCN guideline inclusion in AML — but no data has been disclosed.

floor.headline: Danone's bounce beat the estimate
floor.body_md:
A single mean-reversion setup in Danone (BN.PA) carried the day. RSI at 36.6 and an inflecting MACD, following an 8.64% decline, placed the stock 3.1% below its 20-day SMA with no news catalyst—textbook oversold conditions. The thesis was sound: a 1.3% bounce to clear the French FTT gate.

The position delivered 2.1% instead, closing on schedule rather than signaling reversal. The clean exit suggests momentum carried further than the model's estimate. Whether that reveals an edge in the setup or reflects Friday's particular market conditions won't be clear until Monday. The thesis held. The forecast was tight.

floor.headline: When Yesterday's Winners Close the Door
floor.body_md:
ALHAF.PA was yesterday's strongest mover at +30.50%, today's costliest loss at -15.94%, a £288 deficit that shaped the session. By open, the momentum was exhausted; the stock flatlined through its scheduled exit. FUM.L supplied the sole profit, £210, but under scrutiny it's a penny-stock mirage: at £0.01, the minimum tick equals the quoted price, so zero movement registered as +11.00% in percentage terms.

The rule was mechanical: buy yesterday's winners. Nine of ten trades disproved it today. The bot's thesis assumed prior strength would attract fresh buyers the next session. The market didn't cooperate. The same rule runs tomorrow unchanged. Early question: have gap-driven entries finally lost their edge, or is today just timing noise?

floor.headline: Mid-tier strategies tread water
floor.body_md: Mid-tier strategies muddled through a quiet session. Sector-rotator posted modest gains (+0.27%), while bond-cycle barely moved (+0.04%). Commodity-momentum and news-reactive both slipped -0.35%, the latter suffering across five names including AAL, DELL, and LLY. Macro-aligned bore steeper losses: six trades averaging -1.87%, dragged by semiconductor holdings (LRCX, ASML.AS). Momentum-trader's single trade in SOXL.MI delivered a sharp -14.09% loss. Overall, a day when secondary strategies lacked direction.

floor.headline: MU surged 21% on AI memory re-rating; universe filters cost semiconductor trades
floor.body_md: Micron (MU) soared 21.48% on UBS's AI-memory structural re-rating—price target raised to Street-high $1,625—the session's biggest miss. MU sat outside all active universes entirely, eliminating a pre-gap entry on the May-26 broker note. ON (+9.95%, US-China trade optimism triggered by Nvidia CEO Beijing delegation) and SNDK (+9.20%, anticipatory Russell 1000 addition buying) encountered the same large-cap universe ceiling. TER (+8.11%) continued its AI-testing momentum untraded. The pattern is clear: semiconductor re-ratings and large-cap consumer moves above the bot's universe thresholds are structurally invisible—not missed by signal lag or momentum filters, but by design boundary. Universe bounds, not execution, remain the binding constraint.
